primary_diagnosis.name: Verruca vulgaris (common viral warts, HPV-related), multiple with periungual involvement and autoinoculation spread; benign, no malignant features

differential.evidence_basis: Multiple rough, exophytic, hyperkeratotic dome-shaped papules on the hands/fingers with disrupted dermatoglyphics and pinpoint black dots (thrombosed capillaries), spreading by autoinoculation over months, is the classic picture of common viral warts.
Also a spreading viral skin infection, but lesions are smooth, dome-shaped and umbilicated with a waxy core, not rough and hyperkeratotic — the verrucous surface argues against it.

The patient's own fear and the reason cutaneous malignancy must be actively screened for — but multiplicity, symmetric benign morphology, thrombosed capillaries and disrupted skin lines all point away from it, and there is no solitary rapidly-growing, bleeding, ulcerated or irregularly-pigmented lesion.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Opening picture is strongly verruca vulgaris. The task is to reassure and screen OUT malignancy on morphology, not to escalate reflexively to exclude cancer with an urgent visit.

differential.diagnosis: verruca vulgaris, multiple with periungual involvement
differential.evidence_basis: No spontaneous bleeding, no dark/irregular pigmentation, no ulceration, no solitary rapidly-enlarging lesion, no lymphadenopathy — confirms benign viral warts and excludes malignant features.

Malignant red-flag features are screened negative (no bleeding, no colour change, no ulceration, no dominant rapidly-growing lesion); classic verrucous morphology with thrombosed capillaries confirms benign warts. Remains the safety-net trigger if morphology later changes.
Argues against: actinic keratoses are flat, sandpaper-like lesions on chronically sun-damaged skin in older patients, not exophytic dome-shaped papules in a younger adult on the dorsal hands.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Once malignancy is screened negative, the correct destination is calm self-care with keratolytic + filing and safety-netting, not urgent in-person assessment or biopsy.

differential.diagnosis: verruca vulgaris (common viral warts), multiple, periungual, spread by autoinoculation; benign
differential.evidence_basis: Characteristic verrucous papules with thrombosed capillaries and disrupted skin lines; malignant and infective complications actively excluded; responds to topical keratolytic plus mechanical paring, with cryotherapy for refractory lesions.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Paring to reveal pinpoint thrombosed capillaries confirms the diagnosis in person, but is not required to commit to management — the clinical/video picture is diagnostic and treatment is not delayed for it.

red_flags.description: Features suggesting cutaneous malignancy (SCC / amelanotic melanoma) — spontaneous bleeding, dark or irregular pigmentation, ulceration, or a single lesion growing much faster than the rest
red_flags.clinical_significance: The one genuinely serious alternative the patient (rightly) fears. At this encounter it is screened negative on morphology, so it is the safety-net trigger rather than a present problem; a lesion that later bleeds, darkens or ulcerates needs in-person dermatological review.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: A true amelanotic melanoma or SCC dismissed as 'just a wart' could be diagnosed late; conversely, over-calling it drives an unnecessary urgent visit and biopsy of a benign wart.

red_flags.description: Secondary bacterial infection / ascending lymphangitis from picking — surrounding warmth, spreading redness, swelling, pus, red streaks up the arm, or fever
red_flags.clinical_significance: Repeated picking and tweezing breaks the skin and can seed bacterial infection; red streaks up the arm indicate lymphangitis needing prompt in-person care. Screened negative at this encounter, so it is a safety-net trigger.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Untreated cellulitis/lymphangitis from an excoriated wart can progress to a spreading soft-tissue infection.

pathophysiology_summary: Verruca vulgaris (the common wart) is a benign epidermal proliferation caused by human papillomavirus (HPV, commonly types 1, 2, 4, 27 and 57) infecting keratinocytes through small breaks in the skin. Viral replication drives epidermal hyperplasia and hyperkeratosis, producing the exophytic, rough, dome-shaped papule with a disrupted dermatoglyphic surface. Dilated, thrombosed dermal capillaries within the lesion appear as the pathognomonic pinpoint black dots ('seeds') seen after paring. Warts spread by autoinoculation — physical trauma such as picking, biting or shaving releases virus into micro-abrasions on adjacent skin, which explains the rapid periungual and multi-digit spread in someone who has been picking at them. Most warts are self-limiting and resolve over months to a couple of years as cell-mediated immunity develops, but treatment (topical keratolytics such as salicylic acid combined with mechanical paring, or cryotherapy) shortens the course. There is no role for systemic antibiotics or antivirals in an uncomplicated wart. The main differentials are other viral (molluscum) or, rarely, malignant (SCC/amelanotic melanoma) lesions, which are distinguished on morphology.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Reading the patient's cancer fear and the note's alarming 'aggressive malignancy' framing as a reason to escalate to an urgent in-person visit or ED — when the picture is classic benign verruca vulgaris manageable with self-care. The source note's urgent after-hours home-visit framing is the trap.

Being swept into the patient's health anxiety and over-investigating (urgent biopsy, urgent dermatology) a clinically characteristic multiple-wart presentation, rather than reassuring and screening malignancy out on morphology.
Prescribing oral antibiotics or systemic antivirals for spreading warts with no signs of bacterial infection — the spread is autoinoculation, not infection requiring systemic drugs.

Treating the warts topically but failing to instruct the patient to STOP picking/biting/tweezing — the single behaviour driving the rapid spread; without it the warts keep multiplying despite treatment.

Correctly reassuring but failing to safety-net the two trajectories that would change management — a lesion that bleeds/darkens/ulcerates (possible malignancy) or surrounding spreading redness with red streaks (secondary infection).

cannot_determine_via_chat: Paring (mechanical debulking) of the hyperkeratotic surface to reveal the pinpoint thrombosed capillaries that confirm the diagnosis
Dermoscopy of any atypical lesion to exclude amelanotic melanoma or SCC
Close tactile assessment of surface texture and disruption of dermatoglyphic lines
Histopathology (skin biopsy) if a lesion were morphologically atypical
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: None urgent. Manage remotely with reassurance, topical keratolytic (salicylic acid) plus mechanical filing/paring self-care and autoinoculation/contagion advice, with a clear malignancy and secondary-infection safety-net; recommend routine GP review in ~2 weeks for consideration of cryotherapy if lesions are refractory. Escalate only if a lesion bleeds spontaneously, darkens/ulcerates, grows rapidly, or the surrounding skin becomes hot, red, swollen or develops ascending red streaks.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Low — multiple classic verrucous papules with thrombosed capillaries and disrupted skin lines are clinically/video-diagnosable. The real risk is direction of error: over-triage/over-investigation of a benign wart driven by the patient's cancer fear, or missing a genuinely atypical lesion or a secondary infection from picking.
